quality.rationale: 投影度规结构清晰，需计算行列式降秩并结合Gram-Schmidt构造正交坐标，逻辑链条完整但属纯几何练习

origin.question:
Problem 6.5. A space purports to be 3-dimensional, with coordinates $x, y, z$ and the metric
$$
\mathrm {d s}^{2} = \mathrm {d x}^{2} + \mathrm {d y}^{2} + \mathrm {d z}^{2} - \left(\frac {3}{1 3} \mathrm {d x} + \frac {4}{1 3} \mathrm {d y} + \frac {1 2}{1 3} \mathrm {d z}\right)^{2}.
$$
Show that it is really a two-dimensional space, and find two new coordinates $\zeta$ and $\eta$ for which the line element takes the form
$$
\mathrm {d s}^{2} = \mathrm {d} \zeta^{2} + \mathrm {d} \eta^{2}.
$$
origin.answer: Space is 2-dimensional. New coordinates: $\\xi = \\frac{12}{5}\\left(\\frac{3}{13}x + \\frac{4}{13}y\\right)$, $\\eta = \\frac{13}{5}\\left(-\\frac{4}{13}x + \\frac{3}{13}y\\right)$, yielding $ds^2 = d\\xi^2 + d\\eta^2$.
origin.solution:
Solution 6.5. We can decide if the space is 3-dimensional or not by evaluating the 3-volume spanned by $\mathrm{d}x\mathrm{d}y\mathrm{d}z$
$$
\begin{array}{l} \mathrm {d V} = \mathrm {g}^{\frac {1}{2}} \mathrm {d x d y d z} \\ = \left| \begin{array}{l l l} 1 - \left(\frac {3}{1 3}\right)^{2} & - \left(\frac {3}{1 3}\right) \left(\frac {4}{1 3}\right) & - \left(\frac {1 2}{1 3}\right) \left(\frac {3}{1 3}\right) \\ - \left(\frac {3}{1 3}\right) \left(\frac {4}{1 3}\right) & 1 - \left(\frac {4}{1 3}\right)^{2} & - \left(\frac {4}{1 3}\right) \left(\frac {1 2}{1 3}\right) \\ - \left(\frac {1 2}{1 3}\right) \left(\frac {3}{1 3}\right) & - \left(\frac {4}{1 3}\right) \left(\frac {1 2}{1 3}\right) & 1 - \left(\frac {1 2}{1 3}\right)^{2} \end{array} \right| d x d y d z = 0. \\
\end{array}
$$
Since this is identically zero for all $x, y, z$ , the 3 coordinates are always linearly dependent. The space is thus either two dimensional or one dimensional. We can now throw away one coordinate, say $z$ , by taking $z =$ constant; this is allowed because it is an "ignorable coordinate," i.e. the coefficients of the metric do not depend on $z$ . The metric left is
$$
\mathrm {d s}^{2} = \mathrm {d x}^{2} + \mathrm {d y}^{2} - \left(\frac {3}{1 3} \mathrm {d x} + \frac {4}{1 3} \mathrm {d y}\right)^{2}.
$$
It is in fact 2-dimensional, not 1-dimensional, because
$$
\mathbf {g} = \det \left( \begin{array}{c c} 1 - \left(\frac {3}{1 3}\right)^{2} & - \left(\frac {3}{1 3}\right) \left(\frac {4}{1 3}\right) \\ \left(\frac {3}{1 3}\right) \left(\frac {4}{1 3}\right) & 1 - \left(\frac {4}{1 3}\right)^{2} \end{array} \right) \neq 0.
$$
It is not difficult to find the coordinate transformation (e.g. by Gram-Schmidt orthogonalization)
$$
\begin{array}{l} \xi = \frac {1 2}{5} \left(\frac {3}{1 3} x + \frac {4}{1 3} y\right) \\ \eta = \frac {1 3}{5} \left(- \frac {4}{1 3} x + \frac {3}{1 3} y\right) \\
\end{array}
$$
which gives $\mathrm{ds}^2 = \mathrm{d}\xi^2 +\mathrm{d}\eta^2$ , obviously the simplest form of the metric. The more sophisticated reader will notice that the original metric was of the form
$$
\mathbf {g}_{\alpha  eta} = \delta_{\alpha  eta} - \mathbf {V}_{\alpha} \mathbf {V}_{ eta},
$$
where $\mathbf{V}_{\alpha}$ is the Euclidean unit vector $\left( rac{3}{13},  rac{4}{13},  rac{12}{13}ight)$ . This metric just projects a 3-dimensional Euclidean space into a 2-dimensional space perpendicular to $\mathbf{V}$ , so it is obvious that there had to exist $\xi$ and $\eta$ coordinates which make it a flat-space 2-dimensional metric.
